instructions: Choose the best answer. After you answer, you'll get an explanation — including why each wrong choice is wrong. Aim to understand the feedback, not just to get a score.

options.text: The public, by direct vote on every law

feedback.correct: Right. Statutes are enacted by the legislature — the branch of elected lawmakers — which is what gives statutes their democratic authority.
feedback.b: Agencies make regulations, not statutes. An agency can only act after a statute has delegated power to it.
feedback.c: Courts decide cases and interpret the law; they do not enact statutes.
feedback.d: Statutes come from the legislature, not from a direct public vote on each law.

questions.prompt: A bill that has passed the legislature and been signed by the executive first becomes a ______, before it is reorganized by subject into the code.

feedback.correct: Right. The freshly enacted law is a session law — the official text as passed — and only later gets codified by subject.
feedback.a: A regulation comes from an agency, not from a bill passing the legislature.
feedback.c: A slip opinion is an early court decision, part of the case process, not the legislative one.
feedback.d: Codification happens after this stage. The question asks what the law is before it is organized into the code — that's the session law.

questions.prompt: Where does an agency get the power to issue a regulation?

options.text: From a court order directing it to act
From a statute that delegates authority to it
From the agency simply deciding the rule is needed

feedback.correct: Right. Agencies have no inherent lawmaking power. A statute must first delegate authority — and that statute also sets the limits of what the agency may do.
feedback.a: Courts review agency action; they are not the source of an agency's rulemaking power.
feedback.c: An agency cannot bootstrap its own authority. Without a statute behind it, a regulation has no force.
feedback.d: Opinions can interpret or invalidate regulations, but they do not grant an agency the power to make them.

questions.prompt: Why does an agency publish a proposed rule before issuing a final rule?

options.text: To give the public a chance to comment before the rule binds anyone
Because proposed rules are more legally powerful than final rules
To ask the legislature for permission to act
Because a court ordered it to publish a draft

feedback.correct: Right. The proposed-rule stage opens a comment period so affected people can respond, and it builds a record the agency must weigh before finalizing.
feedback.b: It's the reverse: the proposed rule is just a draft and binds no one. The final rule is the one with the force of law.
feedback.c: Permission already came from the authorizing statute. The comment process is about public input, not legislative sign-off.
feedback.d: The notice-and-comment step is a normal part of rulemaking, not something triggered by a court order.

questions.prompt: A court's decision as first released — standing alone and still subject to correction — is called a ______.

feedback.correct: Right. The slip opinion is the decision in its earliest form, before it is finalized and published in the official reports.
feedback.a: A bill is a proposed statute, not a court decision.
feedback.c: A session law is an enacted statute, part of the legislative process.
feedback.d: A final rule is an agency regulation, not a court's opinion.

questions.prompt: A lawmaker wants to create an entirely new, jurisdiction-wide program and provide funding for it. Which source is the right tool?

feedback.correct: Right. Creating a broad new program and funding it is exactly what statutes do — only the legislature has that authority.
feedback.a: A regulation can implement a program, but it can't create one from nothing; it needs a statute behind it first.
feedback.c: A court resolves a specific dispute; it cannot stand up a funded, jurisdiction-wide program.
feedback.d: A slip opinion is just an early form of a court decision — same limits as any case, and not a tool for building programs.

questions.prompt: A statute directs an agency to set 'safe exposure limits,' but you need the exact numerical limit. Where do you look?

feedback.correct: Right. The statute set the goal and delegated the detail; the precise number lives in the agency's regulation.
feedback.a: A bill is only a proposal — and the technical limit wouldn't be there anyway; that detail was left to the agency.
feedback.b: The session law is the statute as enacted, which here sets the goal but not the specific number.
feedback.d: A constitution sets broad limits on government power, not specific technical exposure thresholds.

questions.prompt: A statute is silent on whether it covers a brand-new situation, and you need to know how it applies to specific facts. Which source is most likely to answer?

feedback.correct: Right. Courts interpret the law and apply it to concrete facts; a published opinion is where that guidance lives and can be relied on.
feedback.a: A proposed rule is a draft regulation that binds no one, and it won't tell you how a statute applies to your facts.
feedback.c: The session law gives you the enacted text — but the text is exactly what's silent here; you need an interpretation.
feedback.d: The bill is just the earlier proposal; it won't resolve how the enacted law applies to a new situation.
